(a–d) EBSD phase map, where red represents austenite and blue represents ferrite. paper_abstract: a Special Steel Department, Central Iron and Steel Research Institute (CISRI), Beijing, 100081, China b State Key Laboratory of Advanced Special Steel, Beijing, 100081, China
paper_title: Duplex microstructure enhanced mechanical property and underlined mechanism in Fe35MnxAl0.1C low-density steel
citation_sentences: The microstructures of the 4Al, 6Al, 8Al, and 10Al steels after solution treatment are shown by the phase distribution maps in Fig. 1(a–d). EBSD analysis indicates that 4Al steel is a single-phase face-centered cubic $( { \mathrm { F C C } } ) \ Y$ austenite lightweight steel, 6Al steel is an austenite-based duplex lightweight steel, and 8Al and 10Al steels are ferrite-based duplex lightweight steels. It also shows that the microstructures with quasi-equiaxed grains are obtained under the solution treatment conditions. It can be observed that the austenite grains contain numerous annealing twin boundaries (represented by white lines).

Fig. 1(e–h) shows the selected area diffraction patterns (SADPs) of 4Al, 6Al, 8Al, and 10Al steels along the [100]γ zone axis of the austenite grains, where obvious satellite diffraction spots can be seen near the γ diffraction spots. The satellite diffraction spots near the $\gamma$ diffraction spots are related to the concentration fluctuations of Al and C atoms [11, 36], indicating that spinodal decomposition occurred during quenching. Very weak diffraction spots are identified at the {110} positions in 6Al, 8Al, and 10Al steels. In previous studies, this kind of ordered structure is revealed to be an $\mathrm { L } 1 _ { 2 }$ type short-range ordered crystal structure [11,37]. It can be seen that the intensity of these satellite diffraction spots decreases with increasing of the nominal Al content, which is revealed to be an $\mathbf { L } 1 _ { 2 }$ type short-range ordered crystal structure.

Fig. 1(i–k, m) shows the selected area diffraction patterns (SADPs) of 6Al, 8Al and 10Al steels along the $[ 1 1 0 ] _ { \alpha }$ zone axis of the ferrite grains, where distinct superlattice diffraction spots are observed. When h, k and l (Miller indices) are all even, the diffraction spots belong to both B2 and $\mathrm { D O } _ { 3 } .$ When h, k and l are all odd, the diffraction spots belong only to $\mathrm { D O } _ { 3 }$ $[ 1 1 , 1 6 ]$ . Fig. 1(j–l, n) presents the $\mathrm { D O } _ { 3 }$ dark-field (DF) images acquired from the {111} and $\{ 0 0 2 \} _ { \mathrm { D O 3 } }$ diffraction spots in the same field of view for the 6Al, 8Al and 10Al steels, respectively. The green arrows show that the dark-field image acquired from the $\{ 0 0 2 \} _ { \mathrm { D O 3 } }$ reflection exhibits a lower intensity or approximate intensity than that obtained from the $\{ 1 1 1 \} _ { \mathrm { D O 3 } }$ reflection, indicating the coexistence of main $\mathrm { D O } _ { 3 }$ domains in the ferritic matrix. The yellow arrows indicate that the dark-field image acquired from the $\{ 0 0 2 \} _ { \mathrm { D O 3 } }$ reflection exhibits a higher intensity than that obtained from the {111} reflection, indicating the coexistence of $\mathrm { D O } _ { 3 }$ domains and B2 domains within the ferritic matrix. Therefore, $\mathrm { D O } _ { 3 }$ ordered domains coexist within both the B2 ordered domains and the ferrite matrix [23]. The maximum sizes of $\mathrm { D O } _ { 3 }$ ordered domains in the 6Al, 8Al, and 10Al steels are estimated to be approximately 2.0 nm, 2.1 nm, and 19 nm, respectively. The size of the $\mathrm { D O } _ { 3 }$ ordered domains increases with the increase of the nominal aluminum

content, suggesting that Al promotes the precipitation of $\mathrm { D O } _ { 3 }$ ordered domains. In addition, acicular precipitates are observed within the ferrite grains of the 10Al steel, as shown in Fig. 1(o and p). It can be observed that Mn and C are significantly enriched in the acicular precipitates, while Al is depleted. The precipitates are identified as 18R martensite formed within the ferrite of the Fe-Mn-Al-C alloy during quenching [38–41]. This type of acicular martensite can significantly reduce the ductility of ferrite [42]. The TEM EDS mapping analysis indicates that the 18R martensite is rich of manganese and carbon, as displayed in Fig. 1(p).

Based on above analysis on the microstructure of the low-density steels with different Al content as shown in Fig. 1, it can be concluded that the microstructure of the low-density steel is strongly affected by the aluminum content, giving nearly full austenite microstructure for 4 %Al and 6 %Al,ferrite based duplex microstructure for 8 %Al, but nearly full ferrite microstructure for 10 % AL. Furthermore, in the austenite phase, the short-range ordering of Al and C could be found in the austenite phase of the low-density steel with aluminum evolved from 6 % to 10 %. In the ferrite phase, both B2 and $\mathrm { D O } _ { 3 }$ could be found in the ferrite phase, indicating that both ordered phases are easily developed in the ferrite phase.

citation_sentences: X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was conducted to further examine the phase composition and mass fractions in the lightweight steels, as presented in Fig. 2(a). The results indicate that the mass fractions of ferrite are 75.7 % and 98.1 % for 8Al and 10Al steels, respectively. The microhardness of austenite and ferrite in the designed steels is shown in Fig. 2(b). It can be seen that the microhardness of ferrite is significantly higher than that of austenite, indicating that ferrite acts as a strengthening phase in the designed steels.

citation_sentences: The typical tensile engineering stress-strain curves and mechanical properties of 4Al, 6Al, 8Al, and 10Al steels are shown in Table 3 and Fig. 3(a and b). It can be observed that as the Al content increases from 4 wt% to 8 wt%, the yield strength and tensile strength increase from 239 MPa to 552 MPa–455 MPa and 740 MPa, respectively, while the uniform elongation only decreases from 38 % to 31 %. However, as the Al content increased up to 10 %, the yield strength is increased to 585 MPa, but the tensile elongation is only about 4.6 %, exhibiting the brittle characteristics. As demonstrated in Section 3.1, the degradation in plasticity of the 10Al steel can be attributed to the coarsening of $\mathrm { D O } _ { 3 }$ ordered domains [15] and the formation of 18R martensite [42].

based duplex steels [43,44]. Fig. 3 (e) presents the evolution of microhardness with strain for the 4Al and 8Al steels it can be observed that the microhardness of austenite in both steels increases with increasing strain. However, the microhardness of ferrite in the 8Al steel initially decreases and then increases with strain. That is, the ferrite in the 8Al steel exhibits softening in the early stages of deformation, indicating differences in the deformation mechanisms between ferrite and austenite in the 8Al steel.

Fig. 3(d) reveals that 8Al steel exhibits multi-stage strain hardening, which is associated with the dynamic slip band refinement mechanism in ferrite. In stage A, the movement of dislocations leads to a sharp decrease in the strain hardening rate. In stage B, a reduction in the strain hardening rate is caused by the slip plane softening effect induced by dislocation shearing of the $\mathrm { D O } _ { 3 }$ long-range ordered domains [13]. In stage $\mathrm { C } ,$ the passing stress generated by slip bands of stored dislocations has been demonstrated to be the primary contributor to strain hardening [12]. With the increase of strain, the number of slip bands increases, the spacing between slip bands decreases, and the passing stress rises, which leads to an increase in the strain hardening rate. In stage D, with the continued increase of strain, the slip bands become nearly saturated, and cross-slip of dislocations is activated, which leads to a decrease in the strain hardening rate. In stage $\mathrm { E } ,$ localization of stress results in a rapid decline in the strain hardening rate and leads to plastic failure.

citation_sentences: The microstructure of the uniform deformation zone of the experimental steels after tensile deformation is shown in Fig. 4. Due to the plastic instability of the 10Al steel, the microstructures after fracture at \~3 % engineering strain are shown in Fig. 4(g–i). It can be observed that the deformation mechanisms of austenite $( \mathrm { F i g } , 4 ( \mathbf { a } , \mathbf { b } , \mathbf { d } , \mathbf { e } , \mathbf { g } ) )$ and ferrite (Fig. 4(c–f, h, i)) are distinctly different. The microstructure of the austenite after tensile deformation is characterized by dislocation cell blocks regardless of the aluminum content, as shown in Fig. 4(a, b, d), which is common in low-carbon or low-aluminum lightweight steels [1, 45,46]. Additionally, a small number of deformation twins are observed in 8Al steel (Fig. 4(e)), which is associated with the localization of stress [1,47]. Fig. 4 (g) shows that the 10Al steel exhibits complex planar slip characteristics during the early deformation stage of austenite, which is similar to the deformation structure of the 6Al steel reported in our previous study [1]. The difference in dislocation deformation structure between this kind of low carbon austenitic lightweight steel and the traditional high carbon austenitic lightweight steel may be associated with the carbon induced suppression of cross-slip [46] and the development of the ordered structure [11].

It is very interesting that the dislocations in ferrite exhibit distinct planar slip characteristics, as shown in Fig. 4(c–f, h, i), which is quite rare in BCC structures [20,21]. It can be observed that the ferrite dislocation structure in 8Al steel exhibits a more pronounced planar slip characteristic compared with that in 6Al steel, indicating that the increase in Al content may promote planar slip of ferrite dislocations. However, the increase in Al content also makes the 10Al steel particularly brittle. Fig. 4(h, i) shows that only a small number of long straight dislocations are activated in the ferrite matrix. These long straight dislocations are identified as screw dislocations based on the discussion in Section 4.1. Brittle fracture caused by the activation of only a few long straight screw dislocations has also been observed in the TiZrHfNb0.4 BCC high-entropy alloy [20]. The planar slip of dislocations in ferrite will be discussed in detail in Section 4.2.

Microstructures of austenite $( \mathbf { a } , \mathbf { d } , \mathbf { f } , \mathbf { g } , \mathbf { i } { \mathbf { - k } } )$ and ferrite $( \mathbf { b } , \mathbf { c } , \mathbf { e } , \mathbf { h } ,$ l) in 8Al steel at different engineering strains. paper_title: Duplex microstructure enhanced mechanical property and underlined mechanism in Fe35MnxAl0.1C low-density steel
citation_sentences: To further understand the deformation mechanism of this ferritebased lightweight steel, the microstructures of 8Al steel at different engineering strains are characterized, as shown in Fig. 5.

When the engineering strain reaches 3 %, planar dislocation slip along the slip planes and distinct cross-slip are observed in the austenite $\left( \mathrm { F i g . ~ 5 ~ ( a ) } \right)$ , while the ferrite is primarily characterized by a planar dislocation structure (Fig. 5 (b)). Fig. 5(c) presents a magnified view of the region marked in Fig. 5(b), where it can be observed that edge dislocations, mixed dislocations, and long straight screw dislocations are activated in ferrite of 8Al steel and form slip bands, which is different from the local screw dislocations in ferrite of 10Al steel. As the strain further increases to 10 %, cross-slip of dislocations in austenite becomes more pronounced (Fig. 5(d)), and the slip bands composed of dislocations in ferrite continue to evolve (Fig. 5(e)). When the engineering strain further increases to 20 %, the dislocation cell blocks (Fig. 5(f)) and dislocation cells (Fig. 5(g)) are formed in the austenite, and the slip bands in the ferrite are further refined (Fig. 5(h)). When the engineering strain reaches 28 %, the dislocation cell blocks (Fig. 5(i)) and dislocation cells (Fig. 5(j)) in the austenite are further refined, and a small number of deformation twins are formed due to the localization of stress (Fig. 5 (k)) [1]. Fig. 5(l) displays the dislocation structure in ferrite at 28 % engineering strain, revealing continued refinement of slip bands along with distinct cross-slip of dislocations. The orientation dependence of dislocation cell blocks and dislocation cell substructures in austenite has been analyzed in detail in the literature [1,45,46]. The average slip band (SB) spacing, dislocation cell block (DCB) size, and dislocation cell (DC) size in austenite, as well as the average slip band spacing in ferrite at different deformation stages, are statistically analyzed, as shown in Fig. 6.

Fig. 5(l) displays the dislocation structure in ferrite at 28 % engineering strain, revealing continued refinement of slip bands along with distinct cross-slip of dislocations. paper_title: Duplex microstructure enhanced mechanical property and underlined mechanism in Fe35MnxAl0.1C low-density steel
citation_sentences: There are significant differences in the micro-deformation behavior in ferrite of 10Al steel and 8Al steel, which are closely related to the activation of different types of dislocations. Here, the two-beam diffraction mode has been employed to investigate the types of dislocations in ferrite of 10Al steel and 8Al steel. To better observe the dislocations, the samples are pre-deformed to the 3 % engineering strain. Fig. 7(a–f) presents the dislocation structures in ferrite of 10Al steel using the different g diffraction vectors near the [111] zone axis.

To identify the Burgers vector b of the ferrite, the g⋅b analyses are conducted. When g = [10 1] and $g = [ 1 \overline { { 1 } } 0 ]$ operating vector, some long straight dislocations systematically lose contrast (Fig. 7(a and b)), whereas when $g = [ 0 \mathrm { ~ \overline { { 1 } } ~ }$ 1] operating vector, all dislocations are visible (Fig. 7(c)). It can be concluded that the Burgers vector b is determined to be a/2[111] by the $g \cdot b = 0$ analysis of the ferrite in 10Al steel, as shown in Fig. 7(a–c). Similarly, the Burgers vector of dislocations in ferrite of 8Al steel is also analyzed near the [111] zone axis, as shown in Fig. 7 (g–l). Some dislocations systematically lost contrast under the [0 1 1] and [1 10] operating vector conditions. Subsequently, the Burgers vector b is also determined to be a/2[111] through the g⋅b = 0 analysis of the ferrite in 8Al steel.

Fig. 7(a–l) shows that the dislocation structures are quite different although the Burgers vectors of dislocations in ferrite of both 10Al steel and 8Al steel are of the a/2 < 111 > type. A predominant fraction of the dislocations in the ferrite of 10Al steel are identified as long straight screw dislocations, and their distribution is non-uniform, as shown in Fig. 7(a–f). These long straight screw dislocations are commonly observed in Fe-Al ordered alloys [14–16,48], which can be attributed at least partially to the significant energy difference between edge and screw dislocations, where the energy of screw dislocations is notably lower than that of edge dislocations [14].

Fig. 7(j–l) displays the dislocation structures at the ferrite-austenite phase interface. It can be observed that edge dislocations are more readily activated at the grain boundaries, which can be explained by the high stress present at these boundaries. It is reported that back stresses are generated in the soft phase, and forward stresses are developed in the hard phase during the deformation of heterogeneous structures [50]. In this case, the high stresses at ferrite grain boundaries, together with the stress-multiplication effect [51], facilitate the collective glide of dislocations on the same slip plane. Additionally, approximately 24.3 % austenite in 8Al steel, acting as a soft phase, can effectively alleviate stress localization within the ferrite and enhance the deformation of the ferrite to higher stresses. It is particularly interesting that edge, mixed, and screw dislocations in ferrite all exhibit a typical planar slip characteristic, which will be discussed in Section 4.2.

citation_sentences: sized $\mathrm { D O } _ { 3 }$ ordered domains in 10Al steel cannot be completely sheared (Fig. 8(c)), while the $\mathrm { D O } _ { 3 }$ ordered domains in 8Al steel can be completely sheared along the dislocation line direction and the slip plane direction (Fig. 8(f)). When the $\mathrm { D O } _ { 3 }$ ordered domains are destroyed by dislocations, anti-phase boundaries (APBs) are left behind [16]. The attractive force generated by APB energy leads to the pairing of dislocations. Studies on Fe-Al alloys demonstrate that APB energy increases with Al content [14,55]. Consequently, the APB-induced attractive force in ferrite of 10Al steel becomes substantially stronger than that in ferrite of 8Al steel, resulting in more pronounced dislocation pairing in the 10Al alloy. Once the $\mathrm { D O } _ { 3 }$ ordered domain is completely sheared, no anti-phase boundaries (APBs) will be generated by subsequent dislocations [13]. The size of the superlattice ordered domains can be estimated by multiplying the number of paired dislocations by the Burgers vector [12].

Fig. 8(b, e) shows that the number of dislocation pairs in 10Al steel and 8Al steel usually does not exceed 6 pairs and 3 pairs, respectively. With paired dislocations counts of 12 in 10Al steel and 6 in 8Al steel, the ordered domain sizes in 10Al and 8Al steels are estimated to be 3.0 nm and 1.5 nm, respectively. It is evident that the calculated $\mathrm { D O } _ { 3 }$ ordered domain size in ferrite of 10Al steel is significantly smaller than the TEMobserved value of 19 nm, whereas the calculated size for 8Al steel is very close to its TEM-observed value of 2.1 nm. Consequently, the ordered domains in ferrite of 8Al steel are more readily sheared, whereas the large-sized $\mathrm { D O } _ { 3 }$ ordered domains in ferrite of 10Al steel are difficult to be completely sheared.

To further illustrate the interaction between dislocations and the $\mathrm { D O } _ { 3 }$ ordered structure, inverse fast Fourier transforms (IFFT) are performed on the high-resolution images at positions 1 and 2 in Fig. 8(g) along the (01 1) and (0 1 1) planes, as shown in Fig. 8(h and i). It can be observed that dislocations are composed of a multitude of dislocation dipoles, and the $\mathrm { D O } _ { 3 }$ superlattice is sheared along dislocation line and slip plane by a large number of these dislocation dipoles. These findings are in agreement with observations reported in Refs. $[ 1 2 , 1 3 , 3 0 , 3 1 ]$ where sheared LRO clusters are proposed to cause planar slip.

citation_sentences: In order to calculate the coefficient of K in the austenite phase, Fig. 9 (a) presents the relation (8) for the 4Al steel, from which the similitude constant K is obtained as $K _ { S B + D C B } = 2 . 0 3 , K _ { D C } = 2 . 0 2$ . This value is very close to the reported K values of 2.0–2.9 for austenitic stainless steel [63, 64]. The austenite phases in 4Al and 8Al steels possess similar compositional systems and deformation structures. Studies [45,46] have shown that the similarity coefficient K essentially remains unchanged with variations in C and Al content for high manganese steels with similar deformation structures. Therefore, the similarity constant of austenite in 8Al steel is approximated by that in 4Al steel.
